sections.heading: Core Concepts
sections.content: A **Stack** is a data structure based on the [[primary|Last-In, First-Out]] (**LIFO**) principle. Like a stack of plates, the last one placed on top is always the first to be removed — operations are only allowed at the [[primary|top]].
items: The **Push** operation places new data onto the [[primary|top]] of the **Stack**
The **Pop** operation removes and retrieves the data at the [[primary|top]] of the **Stack**
The **Peek** operation inspects the [[primary|top]] element without removing it
All these operations have a [[primary|time complexity]] of **O(1)**, and random access to elements in the middle is not permitted

sections.heading: Operation Complexity
sections.content: A Stack only accesses data at one end (the top), requiring no shifting or traversal of other elements — this is why its core operations maintain exceptional efficiency.
items: **Push**: Adds data to the top with no shifting or traversal required; time complexity is [[success|O(1)]]
**Pop**: Removes and returns the top element with no shifting or traversal required; time complexity is [[success|O(1)]]
**Peek**: Views the top element without removing it, accessed directly via pointer; time complexity is [[success|O(1)]]
**Search**: Random access is not supported — elements must be popped one by one to find a target; time complexity is [[warning|O(n)]]

sections.heading: What It Optimizes
sections.content: A Stack excels at single-ended **Last-In, First-Out** operations. However, it does not support random access, so double-ended operations or retrieving elements from the middle will degrade performance significantly — in those cases, a more suitable structure should be used.
items: Great for **state backtracking**: linear scenarios like browser back navigation, **Undo**, or **Depth-First Search (DFS)**
Poor for **First-In, First-Out** access: accessing from the bottom degrades complexity — use a **Queue** or **Deque** instead
Poor for **random access**: cannot directly read or modify middle elements — use an **Array** when frequent positional lookups are needed

sections.heading: Problems It Solves
sections.content: Whenever a problem involves Last-In, First-Out ordering or requires state backtracking, a Stack is the natural solution. If you see a need to store a history and process it in reverse from the most recent state, think Stack.
items: Scenarios requiring a return to a previous state, such as browser back navigation or an editor's **Undo** mechanism
Scenarios where exploration must reverse after reaching a dead end, such as **Depth-First Search (DFS)** on graphs or trees
Expressions requiring operator precedence and bracket matching, such as **arithmetic evaluation** or **infix-to-postfix conversion**
Tracking the pause and resume order of nested tasks, such as the **Call Stack** at the program execution level

sections.heading: Understanding Stack Overflow
sections.content: Beginners implementing **function recursion** frequently encounter [[danger|Stack Overflow]] errors. A dedicated explanation of this concept helps clarify the close relationship between the **Stack** data structure and how system memory works.
items: The system uses the **Call Stack** to record function state — a direct illustration of the link between data structures and memory
When **function recursion** is too deep or forms an infinite loop, each new call pushed onto the stack eventually exhausts available capacity
Once stack usage exceeds the system's preset limit, a [[danger|Stack Overflow]] error is triggered
Once the mechanism is clear, beginners can prevent this by limiting recursion depth or switching to an **iterative approach**

sections.heading: Choosing the Right Tool
sections.content: Beginners often struggle to decide whether to use **list**, **deque**, or **LifoQueue** when performance or thread safety is a concern. A dedicated explanation helps you make the right call.
items: **list**: Tail access is [[success|O(1)]], but lacks thread safety and may incur memory reallocation costs — suitable for simple, single-threaded tasks
**deque**: Both ends are [[success|O(1)]] with no reallocation overhead — the best-performing choice for **single-threaded** use
**LifoQueue**: Built for **multi-threaded** environments with full [[warning|thread safety]], ensuring safe concurrent access

sections.heading: Advanced Variants
sections.content: Different problems often call for different Stack variants. A dedicated explanation helps beginners think beyond a single Stack and apply creative variations to tackle more complex challenges.
items: Use a dual-stack design to implement a [[primary|MinStack]] that retrieves the internal minimum in [[success|O(1)]] time
Implement a **Queue** using two Stacks — two rounds of LIFO produce FIFO behavior
A **Monotonic Stack** maintains elements in ascending or descending order, ideal for problems involving adjacent extremes or boundaries
For high-concurrency scenarios, lock-free structures such as a **Treiber Stack** can ensure thread-safe access
